headline: Penang Hokkien Listed as Endangered Despite 1.2 Million Speakers
context: UNESCO's 2025 language vitality assessment classified Penang Hokkien as 'vulnerable' after finding intergenerational transmission dropped 34% among families in George Town.

cards.big: Penang Hokkien has 1.2 million speakers but is classified as vulnerable.
cards.sub: Among Penangites aged 15-25, only 42% are fluent — down from 76% in their parents' generation.

cards.big: Mandarin has replaced Hokkien as the medium of Chinese education.
cards.sub: SJKC instruction in Mandarin means Hokkien is not transmitted through the school system.

cards.big: George Town's UNESCO World Heritage status is partly based on its Hokkien heritage.
cards.sub: The language that underpins the heritage is declining without any preservation programme.

cards.big: Taiwan invested TWD 1.2B in Hokkien language preservation since 2019.
cards.sub: Malaysia has allocated RM0 for any Chinese dialect preservation.

cards.big: Preserving Hokkien architecture while losing the Hokkien language. Heritage without the people who made it is a museum, not a culture.
Heritage languages need institutional support — community media, elective courses, documentation projects. George Town's UNESCO status provides a framework. What is missing is funding.
